data.title: TCW Transform Supply Chain ETF Q4 2024 Commentary
data.description:
Investment Thesis

The world’s economies are undergoing a monumental transformation, reshoring from global supply chains to comparatively local systems. North America is a unique beneficiary of this shift, which we view as a major investment opportunity.

During the 75 years after World War II, the world embarked on a path of building supply chains focused on minimizing manufacturing costs, often producing components in distant locations. This approach sacrificed resilience and reliability, a trade-off starkly exposed by disruptions like the COVID-19 pandemic, trade wars and an increase in tariffs, geopolitical conflicts and tensions, and natural disasters around the world. Companies have already begun to relocalize their supply chains to North America. SUPP’s thesis is that this intricate transformation of global supply chains requires trillions of dollars in investment. We see this as an incredible opportunity to invest in the enablers and beneficiaries of this transformation.

Performance

Net of fees, SUPP returned -4.1% in Q4 2024 compared to 2.4% for the S&P 500. For the year ending December 31, 2024, SUPP returned 11.0% while the S&P 500 returned 25.0%. Since its inception in February 2023, SUPP has delivered a cumulative return of 26.7% compared to 46.3% for the S&P 500 over the same period.

While the total portfolio did not keep pace with the major equity market indices, our investments in the semiconductor sector generally performed well. Holdings such as TSMC (TSM) and NVIDIA (NVDA) saw increases of 14.0% and 10.6%, respectively in the quarter, as the companies continue to experience more demand than they are able to supply for their most advanced technologies. The semiconductor value chain sits in the center of national security interests and geopolitical tensions and our investments are seeing the benefits of the continued U.S. focus on building out an independent domestic semiconductor supply chain to ensure semiconductor and computer security. GFL Environmental (GFL) also contributed nicely to performance in the quarter with shares up 11.7% as the company provided additional transparency on its process to sell its Environmental Services segment at an attractive valuation implying a core solid waste business that would be valued well below industry-leading peers.

Investments in companies with direct exposure to light commercial and residential construction were detractors from performance during the quarter. Most of the weakness was a function of rates as the 10-year treasury increased by nearly 80 basis points during the quarter despite the Fed cutting its target rate by 100 basis points since mid-September. Higher rates are generally associated with softer light commercial construction activity and our investments in Advanced Drainage (WMS) and Ferguson (FERG) underperformed as a function of this backdrop. Additionally, our investment in Canadian Pacific (CP) declined by 15.2% following threats of tariffs up to 25% on Mexico which represents a large and growing market for the railroad. We view these dynamics as temporary and continue to have strong conviction in these investments and their role in the supply chain reshoring megatrend.

Why Active Thematic ETFs?

The rise of the “Magnificent Seven”* and tech stocks may lead some investors to believe they are well exposed to thematic growth – but the data tells us otherwise. Only 8% of SUPP’s portfolio holdings are captured by the S&P 500.

Investors are too tethered to indices and as a result underexposed to capturing the opportunities presented by these once-in- a-generation economic transformations. These transformations are complex and developing rapidly. As active managers we can drive the greatest returns from these megatrends by reacting quickly to new insights or when facts change.

Market Commentary

The U.S. stock market, as represented by the S&P 500 Index, delivered an impressive return of 25.0% (total return) through 2024. This positive performance was driven by corporate earnings that have exceeded market expectations and a solid macroeconomic backdrop that seemed to support the Fed’s goal to achieve a “soft landing”.

With fourth quarter earnings season kicking off, the S&P 500 is forecasted to see annual EPS growth of nearly 11% in 2024 (UBS, 01/06/2025), although the top-heavy concentration of the index certainly inflates that figure. The “Magnificent 7”, whose market capitalization consistently made up 30%+ of the S&P 500 in 2024, have buoyed EPS growth, with 2024 estimates at 36.2%, and the rest of the companies in the index (the S&P 493), with a much more modest 4.8% estimate.

Looking forward, earnings growth is expected to accelerate in 2025, while dispersion is expected to improve. In 2025, earnings for the S&P 500, Magnificent 7, and S&P 493 are projected to be 12.6%, 19.7%, and 9.2%, respectively, which we believe to be an attractive set up for us as active investors.

At a macro level, the economy appears to be on solid footing, although this had led to a sharp change in expectations for the Fed to continue its cut cycle. The unemployment rate is still historically low (4.1% in December), while average hourly earnings growth is above the historical average of the last two decades (3.9% in December). At the same time, the Fed’s progress on inflation has seemingly stalled (2.9% in December), and expectations for interest cuts in 2025 fell from ~5 cuts of ~120 bps at the start of the quarter, to ~4 cuts of ~43 bps through the end of the quarter (Bloomberg, 01/21/2025).

Finally, the Trump 2.0 administration is a major question mark for the U.S. and global economies. So far, the U.S. equity market has strongly approved of his victory while international markets have provided a more mixed response. Trump is notoriously tough on China and Mexico, and generally champions any movement that puts “America first”, however, his comments have historically often lacked follow-through. Trump and his cohorts have promised to cut regulations that are seen as “bad for [U.S.] business”, which is perhaps one of the main contributors to post-election performance.

In the present environment, we believe it remains prudent to focus on compelling secular trends which are more insulated from economic growth worries. As always, we reiterate our unswerving focus on all-weather businesses which exhibit proven management skill, pricing power, low levels of indebtedness, strong free cash flow generation, and a likely resilience in the face of a pronounced economic slowdown.

For standardized performance and current holdings please visit etf.tcw.com/supp/

The performance data quoted represents past performance. Past performance is no guarantee of future results. The investment return and principal value of an investment will fluctuate so that an investor’s shares, when sold or redeemed, may be worth more or less than their original cost and current performance may be lower or higher than the performance quoted. For the most recent month-end performance, please visit etf.tcw.com/supp/

Important Information

Before investing you should carefully consider the fund’s investment objectives, risks, charges and expenses.

This and other information is in the prospectus, a copy of which may be obtained from etf.tcw.com. Please read the prospectus carefully before you invest.

As with any investment, you could lose all or part of your investment in the Fund, and the Fund’s performance could trail that of other investments. The Fund is subject to certain risks, including the principal risks noted below, any of which may adversely affect the Fund’s net asset value (“NAV”) per share, trading price, yield, total return and ability to meet its investment objective.

The description of TCW ETF’s investment strategy is intended to be representative but may be changed from time to time by TCW, and TCW may alter the information at its discretion. TCW intends to be focused and directed in the selection of opportunities to actively engage with portfolio companies of the Fund. As we seek to deliver on our client’s financial objectives, engagement and active ownership are integral components of TCW’s research and investment process. Our data-informed engagement and active ownership practices achieve several objectives. The information elicited from these practices not only helps improve our fundamental research, but our engagement and active ownership practices may also have positive impacts on the company or other entities by suggesting best practices in addressing critical, financially material issues in areas of sustainability, corporate governance, or executive compensation. In TCW’s view, active ownership improves value and informs future investment decisions.

This letter may contain forward-looking statements, which reflect TCW’s current views with respect to, among other things, TCW’s operations and performance. You can identify these forward-looking statements by the use of words such as “anticipate” “approximately,” “believe,” “continue,” “estimate,” “expect,” “intend,” “may,” “outlook,” “plan,” “potential,” “predict,” “seek,” “should,” or “will,” or the negative version of these words or other comparable words. Forward-looking statements are subject to various risks and uncertainties. Accordingly, there are or will be important factors that could cause actual outcomes or results to differ materially from those indicated in these statements. TCW undertakes no obligation to publicly update or review any forward-looking statement, whether as a result of new information, future developments or otherwise. Portfolio holdings included in this presentation excludes cash.

DEFINED TERMS

Magnificent 7 – The Magnificent Seven stocks are a group of high-performing and influential companies in the U.S. stock market: Alphabet, Amazon, Apple, Meta Platforms, Microsoft, NVIDIA, and Tesla.

INVESTMENT RISKS

TCW Transform Supply Chain ETF (NYSE:SUPP) is subject to the following risks: Supply Chain Risks. Companies supply chains are generally subject to risk such as legislative or regulatory changes; adverse market conditions and/or increased competition; technological developments and changing technology; cyberattacks that may compromise a company’s operations or business; occasional sharp price movements which may result from changes in the economy, fuel prices, labor agreements, exchange rate movements, and insurance costs; pandemics, natural disasters or other crisis; boarder and/or import controls; pent-up /increased demand; mobility restrictions; shortages of product and labor; dependence on intellectual property rights, and potential loss or impairment of those rights; research and development costs; and rapid product obsolescence. Global, regional, or local events, such as changes to trade relations, trade restrictions, and/ or military conflict, may materially disrupt or indefinitely impair the operations of these companies. Activism Risk. An activist investor uses an equity stake in a corporation to put public pressure on a company’s management team and board in order to achieve certain objectives such as the increase of shareholder value through changes in corporate policy or financing structure, or reduction of expenses. Non-Diversification Risk. The Fund is considered to be non-diversified, which means that it may invest more of its assets in the securities of a single issuer or a smaller number of issuers than if it were a diversified fund. Limited History of Operations Risk. The Fund is a new fund with no history of operations for investors to evaluate. Emerging Markets Risk Investments in securities and instruments traded in developing or emerging markets, or that provide exposure to such securities or markets, can involve additional risks relating to political, economic, or regulatory conditions not associated with investments in U.S. securities and instruments. Sustainability Investing Strategy Risk. The Fund intends to incorporate the Adviser’s sustainability investment insights and analysis into its portfolio construction process. The Fund may forgo certain investment opportunities if certain companies and industries that do not meet the Adviser’s criteria. The Fund’s results may be lower than other funds that use different sustainability criteria to screen out certain companies or industries. Concentration Risk. The Fund may be susceptible to an increased risk of loss, to the extent that the Fund’s investments are concentrated in the securities and/or other assets of a particular issuer or issuers, country, group of countries, region, market, industry, group of industries, sector or asset class. Please see the Fund’s Prospectus for more information on these and other risks.

PERFORMANCE

Shares of any exchange traded fund (ETF) are bought and sold at market price (not net asset value (NAV)), may trade at a discount or premium to NAV and are not individually redeemed from the Funds. Brokerage commissions will reduce returns. NAVs are calculated using prices as of 4:00 PM Eastern Time. The closing price is the Mid-Point between the Bid and Ask price as of the close of exchange. Closing price returns do not represent the returns you would receive if you traded shares at other times.

INDEX DISCLOSURES

The Morningstar® U.S. Market Extended TR USD Index℠ is a service mark of Morningstar, Inc. and has been licensed for use for certain purposes by TCW The TCW Transform Systems ETF is not sponsored, endorsed, sold, or promoted by the Morningstar, Inc. or any of its affiliates (all such entities, collectively, “Morningstar Entities”). data.title: AAII Sentiment Survey: Pessimism Surges
data.description:
Pessimism among individual investors about the short-term outlook for stocks increased in the latest AAII Sentiment Survey. Meanwhile, optimism and neutral sentiment decreased. Most of this week’s survey responses were captured prior to President Trump’s tariff announcement on Wednesday, April 2.

Bullish sentiment, expectations that stock prices will rise over the next six months, decreased 5.7 percentage points to 21.8%. Bullish sentiment is unusually low and is below its historical average of 37.5% for the 12th time in 14 weeks.

Neutral sentiment, expectations that stock prices will stay essentially unchanged over the next six months, decreased 4.1 percentage points to 16.3%. Neutral sentiment is unusually low and is below its historical average of 31.5% for the 37th time in 39 weeks. Neutral sentiment was last lower on April 1, 2020 (16.0%).

Bearish sentiment, expectations that stock prices will fall over the next six months, increased 9.8 percentage points to 61.9%. Bearish sentiment is unusually high and is above its historical average of 31.0% for the 18th time in 20 weeks. This is the third-highest bearish sentiment reading in the history of the survey and was last higher on March 5, 2009 (70.3%).

The bull-bear spread (bullish minus bearish sentiment) decreased 15.4 percentage points to –40.2%. The bull-bear spread is below its historical average of 6.5% for the 13th time in 15 weeks.

This week’s special question asked AAII members if they hold gold or other precious metals in their portfolio, either directly or through an exchange-traded fund (ETF).

Here is how they responded:

  • No, I do not: 59.9%.
  • Yes, I own gold or other precious metals: 18.4%.
  • Yes, I own both precious metals and mining companies: 11.9%.
  • I just own mining companies that focus on precious metals: 7.4%.
  • I am considering adding gold to my portfolio: 2.1%.

This week’s Sentiment Survey results:

  • Bullish: 21.8%, down 5.7 percentage points.
  • Neutral: 16.3%, down 4.1 percentage points.
  • Bearish: 61.9%, up 9.8 percentage points.

Historical averages:

  • Bullish: 37.5%.
  • Neutral: 31.5%.
  • Bearish: 31.0%.

The AAII Sentiment Survey has been conducted weekly since July 1987. The survey and its results are available online.

If you want to become an effective manager of your own assets and achieve your financial goals, consider a risk-free 30-day Trial AAII Membership.

data.title: Red Cat Stock: Where Do We 'Reciprocate' The Tariffs?
data.description:
Tariff talks

Red Cat Holdings, Inc. (NASDAQ:RCAT) had an initial spike leading up to President Trump's tariff announcements. In the meantime, from then until now, it has sold off like the rest of the market. Is there really an opportunity? Revenue growth expectations are massive due to US military contracts, the question is, can this company ever turn a positive operating margin? Let's take a look at the most recent earnings report and some comparisons for details.

Year-to-date performance

Off -46.2% this year, Red Cat is not alone in this situation. All the drone stocks are getting hit, as are high multiple tech and periphery stocks in general. This is starting to look similar to the 2022 washout year that saw a big tech bear market combined with a thrashing of overpriced hype stocks in their infancy phases. I do not doubt that drones, like AI and robotics, are one of the most important products of our lifetime, but you can still pay way too much in the beginning.

My last article on drones

In my last article on drones in general as a portfolio theme, Red Cat did not make the list of the top 10 cheapest names I could screen for. The metric I used was a quant-style system ranking of the stocks by book and sales yield.

Being a fan of both Joel Greenblatt and James O'Shaughnessy, the best way to compile data according to their writings on let's say the top 5 plays, would be to combine yields that would be positively correlated with value. This is similar to how a long/short quant system might be set up at a hedge fund, find your top 10/20 most expensive stocks and go short, find your top 10/20 cheapest stocks and go long.

The thesis in using these two yields to rank and score was that book value represents a company able to build equity in lieu of excessive debt (or dilution) and sales yield shows who actually has demand.

Here is an example of some smaller drone companies currently using this factor method:

Stock Book Yield Sales Yield Score Rank
(JOBY) 19.26 0 19.26 3
(AVAV) 24.2 20.9 45.1 2
(KTOS) 28.7 24.4 53.1 1
(RCAT) 4.76 18.16 18.16 4

In this scenario, I used the forward sales numbers for Red Cat, and they still came in below the other 3 up-and-coming drone plays using their TTM sales yields and book values. This is a very expensive stock, and their book yield when compared to the rest of the group is the most troubling aspect. Again, this is the net-worth yield if you will. If a company has huge revenues and lots of intangible assets, maybe this is unimportant. Drone companies are industrial electronic products, not software. Net worth is very relevant.

Red Cat did recently pay down lots of debt but has more financing to come, they used shares as a liquidity source. This still has a net negative effect on book value in a falling share price environment:

The most recent earnings results

The most recent earnings results were initially taken as a positive and some strong sales guidance was the trigger (emphasis added):

-Guidance of $80-$120 million for calendar year 2025, which consists of:

-$25 million in Non-SRR Black Widow sales

-$25 million in Edge 130 sales

-$5 million in Fang FPV sales

-$25 to $65 million in SRR-related Black Widow sales

Our financial position remains solid as we scale to meet increased demand,” added Thompson. “With over $9 million in cash and receivables and the recently secured debt financing of $15 million, we’ve significantly strengthened our capital position heading into a pivotal year. This infusion of non-dilutive capital allows us to aggressively scale production, and meet accelerating demand tied to the U.S. Army’s SRR program and international opportunities. Combined with our strong cash balance and operational discipline, we are confident in our ability to support 2025 revenue guidance and deliver long-term shareholder value.”

Hopefully, the debt financing will allow the company to lay off the gas on share dilution, but the most difficult part for the company is finding a way to turn an operating profit, not finding sales partners.

The gross profit margin is already pretty thin. The operating margin contains the sales and workforce for the most part. Electronics are made overseas for a reason, it's tedious work with a lot of soldering fumes. It's very difficult to make a product in the US of the same quality for a similar wage. Tariffs on overseas goods might help, but the prices of these drones need to increase, or else the company will be forever in a financing loop without positive free cash flow.

Robotics seems to be the only hope to reduce costs for lower dollar value, lower margin small electronic goods.

Palantir partnership

One hope and future catalyst to make these drones at feasible margins is the partnership with Palantir (PLTR):

Partnered with Palantir to deploy Warp Speed, Palantir’s manufacturing OS. This collaboration will transform our supply and manufacturing operations with Palantir’s AI enabled monitoring, process flow enhancement and comprehensive data analysis. Palantir’s Warp Speed will optimize Red Cat’s production and streamline its supply chain, change management, and quality assurance, ultimately reducing costs and improving margins.

This is a solid catalyst for the company, along with the revenue growth that is nearly assured due to government contracts. These two elements are the only things that keep it in the "hold" category above a sell due to having headline catalysts even though the underlying reality looks pretty hard to overcome.

Another catalyst in the balance

The whole reciprocal tariff issue and criticism of the EU not spending its fair share in Defense spending has the union considering whether it wants to buy from the US or just shop locally:

A State Department spokesperson said Trump welcomes recent efforts from European allies to "strengthen their defense capabilities and take responsibility for their own security," but warned against creating new barriers that exclude U.S. companies from European defense projects.

In discussions with commenters in my last article on Red Cat, that was a topic of conversation. The EU would be ramping up Defense spending and feeding the US war machine. Maybe and maybe not. The big weapons producers even find themselves in a pickle, not to mention these smaller drone makers. The EU relationship with China is nowhere near as poor as the China/US relationship. NATO members could still easily source all of their drone needs from China, with or without our support of that.

Summary

I see the catalysts, sales demand from the government combined with Palantir AI supply chain management systems to help improve operating margins. The US government is always a partner to be had with a never-ending money printer, but even they are tightening their wallet. I figured border security would be a bigger issue, but after deportations started, the border seemed calm, not a domestic war zone needing drone swarms.

This is probably one of the best drone stocks to trade due to their headline catalysts, but it still remains even more expensive than the majority of small drone companies focused on government contracts. Kratos and AeroVironment both have positive operating margins because they make a higher dollar value product with better gross margins as well due to higher product differentiation versus competition.

In all honesty, there is nowhere to reciprocate the tariffs on small drones that would help a drone with similar features to cheaper Chinese drones to entice those markets or governments to buy US small drones. Red Cat will have to rely on the government for the foreseeable future, and it will be hard to raise prices. They need to compress margins, somehow.

data.title: Voya Global Bond Fund Q4 2024 Commentary
data.description:
Strategy Overview

Invests in broad global bond sectors including a wide range of debt and derivative securities and currencies.

Portfolio Review

For the quarter ended December 31, 2024, the Voya Global Bond Fund Class I Share underperformed the Index on a net asset value (NAV) basis. Sector and currency allocation contributed over the quarter, while duration and yield curve decisions detracted.

Over the final quarter of 2024, global yields broadly rose and the U.S. dollar strengthened as central bank action and political headlines shaped expectations. U.S. Treasury yields marched higher over the quarter as positive growth and elevated inflation data kept the U.S. Federal Reserve cautious, notably scaling back the number of projected cuts for 2025 and revising 2025 inflation and growth projections higher. Potential future pro-business policies from the incoming administration, including trade tariffs, added to the upward pressure in yields.

In Europe, the European Central Bank (ECB) cut rates amid further progress on inflation but also in the face of lackluster growth. Political turmoil took front stage in Germany and France, and the uncertainty led longer end yields to climb higher.

Inflation data in Japan surprised to the upside, raising expectations for a Bank of Japan (BOJ) rate hike at its December meeting, though the continued cautious stance from the BOJ threw cold water on sentiments as it voted to keep rates unchanged.

In the United Kingdom, the budget proposed in October raised longer term inflation expectations, which was reflected in longer end yields rising. Further, after cutting rates at their November meeting, the Bank of England (BOE) decided to keep rates steady in a more split decision than expected, citing persistent inflationary factors.

As a result, the Bloomberg Global Aggregate Bond Index returned -5.10% for the period. Continued U.S. exceptionalism keeping the Fed cautious and potential tariffs from the incoming administration fueled a stronger U.S. Dollar over the quarter.

Sector and currency allocation contributed over the quarter, while duration and yield curve positioning decisions were detractors for the period. Our duration and yield curve positioning detracted over the quarter as developed markets (DM) rates were higher over the quarter.

Allocation to securitized credit broadly contributed, where commercial mortgage-backed securities (CMBS) was the largest contributor driven by the rally in financial conditions fueling risk appetite.

Allocation to non-agency residential mortgage-backed securities (RMBS) also contributed, where animal spirits post-election combined with positive fundamental factors drove valuations higher over the quarter.

Our exposure to emerging markets (EM) local currencies contributed modestly.

Current strategy and outlook

U.S. policy to create short-term disruptions

Rhetoric from the administration will serve as a negotiating tactic, but actual policy will be watered down to have less of a negative impact. Uncertainty regarding sequencing, magnitude and duration of proposed policies will create short term disruptions, but market reaction will compel the administration to temper policies before they derail growth.

Global growth to be led by United States

U.S. growth will outperform the rest of the globe driven by post-election optimism and investment. Productivity gains from deregulation, innovation, and energy independence support higher potential growth in the U.S., whereas structural challenges in Europe will limit upside. China will provide additional stimulus but desire to limit further accumulation of debt will keep growth weak.

U.S. inflation likely to moderate but remain above Fed’s target

In the near term, inflation will continue to moderate, supported by a re-balanced labor market and a gradual decline in shelter costs, though it will remain above the Fed’s target. However, a policy mix of deficit spending, tariffs and stricter immigration limits will threaten this balance by sustaining a high level of demand while straining supply.

Central bank policies poised to diverge

Central bank policies will become less synchronized due to diverging growth and inflation trends. The Fed will tolerate inflation above its target as they reduce policy rates. The ECB, facing weaker growth and falling inflation, will take rates below neutral. BOJ will be the outlier, raising rates due to inflation concerns.

Markets: Capitalize on dislocations

Higher nominal growth and a positive economic outlook will outweigh extended valuations, supporting growth-dependent assets such as equities and corporate credit spreads in the near term. In the medium term, missteps in policy implementation will likely create periods of market dislocation.

The Bloomberg Global Aggregate Index is an unmanaged index that provides a broad-based measure of the global investment-grade fixed-rate debt markets. The Index does not reflect fees, brokerage commissions, taxes or other expenses of investing. Investors cannot invest directly in an index.

Principal Risks: All investing involves risks of fluctuating prices and the uncertainties of rates of return and yield. Currency To the extent that the Portfolio invests directly in foreign currencies or in securities denominated in, or that trade in, foreign (non-U.S.) currencies, it is subject to the risk that those currencies will decline in value relative to the U.S. dollar or, in the case of hedging positions, that the U.S. dollar will decline in value relative to the currency being hedged. Derivative Instruments Derivative instruments are subject to a number of risks, including the risk of changes in the market price of the underlying securities, credit risk with respect to the counterparty, risk of loss due to changes in interest rates and liquidity risk. The use of certain derivatives may also have a leveraging effect which may increase the volatility of the Portfolio and reduce its returns. Foreign Investments/Developing and Emerging Markets Investing in foreign (non U.S.) securities may result in the Portfolio experiencing more rapid and extreme changes in value than a fund that invests exclusively in securities of U.S. companies, due to smaller markets, differing reporting, accounting and auditing standards, and nationalization, expropriation or confiscatory taxation, foreign currency fluctuations, currency blockage, or political changes or diplomatic developments. Foreign investment risks typically are greater in developing and emerging markets than in developed markets. Asset-Backed (including Mortgage-Related) Securities Defaults on or the low credit quality or liquidity of the underlying assets of the asset-backed (including mortgage-related) securities held by the Portfolio may impair the value of the securities. Credit Derivatives The Portfolio may enter into credit default swaps, either as a buyer or a seller of the swap. As a buyer of the swap, the Portfolio pays a fee to protect against the risk that a security held by the Portfolio will default. As a seller of the swap, the Portfolio receives payment(s) in return for its obligation to pay the counterparty an agreed upon value of a security in the event of a default of the security issuer. Credit default swaps are largely unregulated and susceptible to liquidity, credit, and counterparty risks. Other risks of the Portfolio include but are not limited to: Leverage, Liquidity, Other Investment Companies, Call, Credit, High-Yield Securities, Prepayment, and Extension and Securities Lending. Investors should consult the Portfolio’s Prospectus and Statement of Additional Information for a more detailed discussion of the Portfolio’s risks. An investment in the Portfolio is not a bank deposit and is not insured or guaranteed by the Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation, the Federal Reserve Board or any other government agency.

The strategy employs a quantitative investment process. The process is based on a collection of proprietary computer programs, or models, that calculate expected return rankings based on variables such as earnings growth prospects, valuation, and relative strength. Portfolio construction uses a traditional optimizer that maximizes expected return of the portfolio, while managing tracking error. Data imprecision, software or other technology malfunctions, programming inaccuracies and similar circumstances may impair the performance of these systems, which may negatively affect Fund performance. Furthermore, there can be no assurance that the quantitative models used in managing the Fund will perform as anticipated or enable the Fund to achieve its objective. The strategy is available as a mutual fund or variable portfolio. The mutual fund may be available to you as part of your employer sponsored retirement plan. There may be additional plan level fees resulting in personal performance that varies from stated performance. Please call your benefits office for more information. Variable annuities and group annuities are long-term investments designed for retirement purposes. If withdrawals are taken prior to age 59½, an IRS 10% premature distribution penalty tax may apply. Money taken from the annuity will be taxed as ordinary income in the year the money is distributed. An annuity does not provide any additional tax deferral benefit, as tax deferral is provided by the plan. Annuities may be subject to additional fees and expenses to which other tax-qualified funding vehicles may not be subject. However, an annuity does provide other features and benefits, such as lifetime income payments and death benefits, which may be valuable to you. Variable investments, of any kind, are not guaranteed and are subject to investment risk including the possible loss of principal. The investment return and principal value of the security will fluctuate so that when redeemed, it may be worth more or less than the original investment. In addition, there is no guarantee that any variable investment option will meet its stated objective. All guarantees are based on the financial strength and claims paying ability of the issuing insurance company, who is solely responsible for all obligations under its policies. Insurance products, annuities, and funding agreements issued by Voya Retirement Insurance and Annuity Company (“VRIAC”), One Orange Way, Windsor, CT 06095, which is solely responsible for meeting its obligations. Plan administrative services provided by VRIAC or Voya Institutional Plan Services, LLC (“VIPS”). Securities distributed by or offered through Voya Financial Partners, LLC (“VFP”) (member SIPC)or other broker-dealers with which it has a selling agreement. Only Voya Retirement Insurance and Annuity Company is admitted and can issue products in the state of New York. All companies are members of Voya Financial.

This commentary has been prepared by Voya Investment Management for informational purposes. Any opinions expressed herein reflect our judgment and are subject to change. data.title: Ambarella: A Strong Financial Position Will Help It Survive The Uncertainty
data.description:
Introduction

Ambarella, Inc. (NASDAQ:AMBA) recently reported its full-year results, so I wanted to check its performance and what it’s got in store for the future, now that we also saw a decent decline in shares in the last month or so. Heavy R&D investments I view as positive, and with a great financial position, I can see the company coming out of this uncertainty stronger. However, I don’t think we are there yet, therefore, I am assigning it a Hold rating, until we see what is happening in the global economy.

Briefly on Performance

Starting from the top, the company’s sales were around 25% higher y/y, with impressive growth seen in the latest quarter of 62% compared to the same time last year. The impressive growth can be attributed to the record demand for its edge AI processors. The company shipped over 30m edge AI processors in 2024. Now, more than 70% of the company’s total revenues come from these processors.

In terms of efficiency and profitability on a GAAP basis, these numbers are not where any company wants them to be, however, it is understandable that they are deep in the red because of where the company is in its cycle currently. It is still a relatively young company, and it is spending large sums of its capital on research and development every year, which should yield benefits in the long run. However, it also carries quite a bit of risk if the excessive capital spent on R&D leads to inferior product still. At least the company’s gross margins are quite healthy. There is no point in looking at any other profitability and efficiency metrics since they will all be red.

Now, let’s take a look at the company’s financial position. AMBA finished the year with $144.6m in cash and equivalents, as well as $105.6m in marketable securities, which the company around $8m in interest income every year. Additionally, the company, like many in the semiconductor industry doesn’t hold any debt on its books, which allows the company to focus all the cash flow on what matters, which is furthering the growth of the company. This position is a lot less risky than a company with a massive debt pile. Therefore, the company should be able to continue to operate much longer, as long as it is bringing in enough cash flow to support its operations. The $250m cash cushion will help it tremendously in the future.

It is important to look at the company’s cash burn rate too in this situation. According to this website, the company’s cash burn rate was -$3.8m for the year, which means that the company has a lot of years left in operation with such a high cash position on its books. Therefore, I am not worried about its immediate future, or even in the long run.

Overall, I think the company is going in the right direction. It still has a long way to go before it turns profitable but given its financial position, the company has many years of continuing innovation, which can make or break its future.

Comments on the Outlook

So, now that we know the company is all in on R&D, what has the company produced recently that helped its operations, and what kind of innovative products are they cooking up in the future?

We saw that in the latest quarter, the company’s revenues saw an impressive 62% growth, which means the company’s products are very much in high demand.

The company’s CV Flow 3.0 AI SoCs and N1 series were the standouts this past year, which brought generative AI capabilities to edge devices. The company also already integrated the new low-cost Chinese AI model DeepSeek, from Hangzhou DeepSeek Artificial Intelligence Co., Ltd. (DEEPSEEK) R1, which is driving a lot of the demand for the company’s products.

For the upcoming year, the company expects to ramp its 5nm products like the CV5 and CV7 families as well as continue to expand GenAI capabilities on edge devices through the N1 SoC series, which has the capability to support large language models, or LLMs, like Llama-2. Even with such a tough comparison like the end of 2024, the management still expects to see mid-to-high teens revenue growth for the next year, which tells us that the adoption of the company’s new tech is still very much in high demand. However, it is a bit slower than what we saw at the end of this year, which was around 25%.

The semiconductor industry is very competitive. Many companies like AMBA are spending millions on innovations, which means AMBA cannot drop the ball right now or risk being overtaken by its competitors. I think the company has the know-how to perform well in the future in terms of coming up with even more efficient chips that are integrated even better with all the AI capabilities.

The company is currently developing 2nm technology, which is expected to provide significant improvements in power efficiency and die size reduction. There are two projects currently underway, that target the IoT space and cameras. The release of this chip is anticipated to be at the end of the year if everything goes as planned. We can see that the aggressive investments in R&D are not going to waste and that is one area that I like companies to invest heavily in because according to research, there is a positive correlation between higher R&D spend and higher market share, corporate performance, innovation, and profitability. In other words, the more a company spends on innovation in terms of R&D, the better it will be positioned in the future in terms of higher market share and eventually will be a lot more profitable.

It is not all positive in my view, though. The excess R&D spending, which half of it is actually in share-based compensation means there will be a lot more share dilution going forward, which is quite harmful for current shareholders. Over the last year, the company’s share count increased by around 3.5%, which is not too much, however, with the current share performance taking a huge hit, the current shareholders took a double beating. I don’t see how the company is going to stop share-based compensation any time soon. It just cannot afford to, so expect further dilution in shares leading to an even smaller share of the pie.

In my view, this is necessary for the growth of the company. However, it may seem a bit dire right now because the share dilution is accompanied by an absolute obliteration of its share price. Since peaking at around $85 just a month and a half ago, the company’s shares are down over 46% because of an ongoing tariff war, that sent the company’s shares down another 12% as of writing this article after the Trump Liberation Day “reciprocal” tariffs.

Speaking of tariffs, a lot of the company’s revenues are coming from Taiwan, which is a powerhouse in chip manufacturing and is facing a 32% tariff as of yesterday. However, semiconductors are currently exempt from the reciprocal tariffs, which didn’t stop semiconductor stocks from plunging in share prices. Now, it doesn’t mean that there won’t be tariffs on semis coming. The president hinted at such tariffs before, up to 25% specifically. So, there is still a lot of uncertainty. The company faces indirect impact from its customers, especially in the automotive industry. Since the company’s chips are widely used in automotive systems, the 25% tariff on auto imports of vehicles and parts may reduce the demand for its chips in the future.

In general, the semiconductor industry is back in the negative sentiment once again. This happened back in 2022 and 2023, which followed a massive turnaround this past year. The sector is very cyclical and can be heavily influenced by economic uncertainty, especially when there is a lot of fear of recession looming.

Closing Comments

We are in an environment that is very uncertain right now. There will be consequences of these “reciprocal” tariffs from other countries, which then will lead to even more “reciprocal” tariffs from the U.S. Nothing is out of the question, especially when it comes to how far the new administration is willing to take this war. Semiconductor tariffs are next, in my opinion, and I expect there will be a lot more downside left, especially after the recent AI surge that pushed so many companies to new heights.

Some more capitulation appears to be in the cards. However, given AMBA’s strong financial position and aggressive innovation, I could see the company coming out strong after the dust had settled. I just don’t think it is the right time to start catching the falling knives, even after a 46% decline in shares. It is definitely more enticing now; however, I will practice patience. Some of it is due to my capital already locked up in other stocks and some of it is due to the massive question mark in the global economy.

data.title: Bear Market Is Underway: How Bad Will It Get?
data.description:
President Donald Trump has announced a package of “reciprocal tariffs” that are historically unprecedented. The weighted average increase in the announced tariffs amounts to more than 20%, which is greater than the Smoot-Hawley tariffs of the early 1930s. China's tariff rate will be an astounding 54%.

Unless negotiations are able to quickly bring the tariffs down, a US business cycle recession - and a broader global recession - is very likely to unfold. As a result, a bear market decline of more than -20% is extremely likely. Due to the extremely high starting valuations, this bear market in US equities could be extremely severe.

In this article, we will lay out possible scenarios for a bear market in US equities.

Five Scenarios for Earnings and PE Multiples

In Figure 1 we present five different scenarios for normalized EPS and the trough PE multiple.

Normalized EPS. Normalized EPS estimates are based on the discounted value of prior peak forward EPS at recovery. In other words, the longer it takes for EPS to recover to its peak forward EPS (current), the more the recovery value of future EPS is discounted. We present five scenarios.

PE Multiple. We present five scenarios for the PE multiple of normalized forward EPS. Long-term average historical forward EPS is roughly 16 during the past 30 years. Average forward EPS is roughly 13 going back to 1960. Bear markets typically trough at a multiple that's significantly below the average of the prior 30 years. The discount to the average PE has been strongly correlated to the extent of the recession, the decline in earnings and the time to EPS recovery.

Figure 1: S&P 500 Drawdown Scenarios

Central scenario. As can be seen, a “central” scenario would be for the US economy to experience a mild recession and for the S&P 500 PE on normalized EPS to trough at a value of 15. This would imply an S&P 500 valuation of 2925 - a drawdown of 52.4% from the Feb. 19, 2025, peak close of 6144.15.

Optimistic scenario. The most optimistic scenario that, we believe, is reasonable, under current circumstances, would be to assume no impact to our GAAP EPS estimate as of March 31, 2025, and to apply a PE multiple of 18, which is still significantly above the 30-year historical average. This would imply an S&P 500 value of 4140, representing a drawdown of 32.6%. The decline of the PE ratio under this relatively optimistic scenario would be due to: A) A substantial increase in uncertainty regarding future EPS growth, causing a rise in the implied equity risk premium, B) a decline in medium- and long-term expectations of abnormal earnings growth.

Pessimistic scenario. Under a scenario of a severe recession, a steep decline in normalized EPS (due to a prolonged recovery of peak EPS) and a major increase in the equity risk premium impacting the PE multiple, we see a potential downside scenario of 2100. This would imply a drawdown of 65.8%. This would represent 12 times EPS on normalized EPS of $175. Note that this PE ratio is still significantly above trough PE ratios (on normalized peak EPS) in previous severe recessions and bear markets. Historically, PE ratios have troughed below 10 on normalized peak EPS.

Could it Get That Bad?

I highly encourage readers to carefully study my most recent article where I lay out a great deal of evidence regarding the extent to which S&P 500 profitability, growth and valuations have become extremely dependent on the globalization process. This globalization process really took off shortly after China’s admission into the WTO in 2001. In this article, I establish the following facts about S&P profitability, earnings growth and valuations in the post-globalization period.

1. S&P profit margins - EBIT and operating - expanded spectacularly.

2. Foreign revenue and margins. Virtually all of that profit margin expansion was due to the growth of foreign revenue and margins of the largest and most globalized companies in the S&P 500.

3. The expansion of S&P 500 PE multiples during the past two decades has been largely a function of:

A. The expansion of profit margins.

B. Corresponding increases in returns on capital.

C. Associated increase in EPS growth.

D. Almost all of the above are directly attributable to revenue growth outside of the US, and particularly profit margin expansion outside of the US enjoyed by multinational S&P 500 companies.

The intensification of globalization has been the single most important factor in the extraordinary profitability, earnings growth and PE expansion enjoyed by shareholders of S&P 500 firms during the past 25 years.

Therefore, a partial reversal of the globalization process - if it were to indeed occur - can be expected to cause a partial reversal of the positive trends in profitability, earnings growth and valuation that US investors have grown accustomed to.

Under such circumstances, it's hardly a stretch to suggest that S&P 500 forward PE ratios could revert towards pre-globalization historical norms of 13 to 16 times forward earnings.

The Technical Backdrop

For those that read my articles on March 16 and March 30, it now seems likely that the Market will transition fairly quickly from a Category 2 scenario (support at 5400) to a Category 3 correction scenario (support down to 5117) and relatively quickly proceed to a Category 4 scenario (below 4923) that marks the beginning of a bear market.

I previously believed that relatively moderate tariff announcements could create uncertainty, but little conviction, about a possible business cycle recession. However, it's now my view that the severity of the announced tariffs are likely to generate a high level of conviction about a recession to develop among most market participants very soon.

Figure 2: S&P 500 Pattern Analysis

Sectors Most Impacted

The sectors that will be most adversely impacted by the partial reversal of globalization will be many of the sectors that have the highest exposure to foreign sales.

Figure 3: Sectors With Most Foreign Sales Exposure

By far, the sector that will be most adversely impacted by tariffs and the overall partial reversal of globalization will be information technology. Other sectors such as industrials, communication services, consumer discretionary and consumer staples will be also been extremely impacted.

Stocks such as Apple (AAPL) are highly vulnerably to a major decline, as I point out in this article. Qualcomm (QCOM), Broadcom (AVGO), Meta (META), Alphabet (GOOG)(GOOGL) and Nvidia (NVDA) are also extremely vulnerable to major declines of 50% or more.

Energy and materials sectors will be significantly impacted. However, due to tariff exemptions for many products in this sector (e.g. crude oil and iron ore), we think the sector will be somewhat less impacted.

Sectors such as real estate, financials, healthcare and utilities will be relatively less impacted by tariffs and other factors that will be associated with partial deglobalization.

Conclusion

The partial reversal of globalization will cause slowing of sales growth, increased costs, contraction of profit margins, reduction of returns on capital and a lower rate of EPS growth. As a result, PE ratios are likely to contract significantly.

If PE ratios contract toward their historical norms, the S&P 500 may decline by -50% or more from a high of Feb. 19, 2025.

While a bear of a market has not yet been confirmed, we believe that one is currently underway and that the S&P 500 index will ultimately fall considerably further from current levels.

Final Thoughts

In a partially de-globalizing world, and a world of AI-led disruption, investors need to take a radically different approach to how they manage their portfolios. Active management and out-of-the-box strategies will be necessary. At Successful Portfolio Strategy, we're preparing to take advantage of major disruptions related to the deglobalization process. Regardless of political inclinations, we cannot wish away the pain that most investors will experience. Massive economic and market disruptions will cause losses for most investors. However, it will also mean massive opportunity for a few investors that understand the new world and are prepared to act decisively.

data.title: Introducing A Time-Tested Solution For Municipals
data.description:
By Kevin Flanagan & Rick Harper

With U.S. interest rates returning to a more ”normal” setting, bond buyers are presented with a landscape for fixed income investing that a generation of investors may not have witnessed before. However, the same rules still apply when trying to discern value whether we are in a rising, falling or stable rate environment. One time-tested approach for fixed income investing is the laddered bond strategy and WisdomTree now offers two new solutions for the municipal bond market. Today, in collaboration with Insight Investment WisdomTree launched the WisdomTree Core Laddered Municipal Fund (WTMU) and the WisdomTree High Income Laddered Municipal Fund (WTMY), two ETFs designed to bring a modern, actively managed laddered approach to the municipal bond market. In this blog post, we’ll outline how these strategies aim to help investors navigate the muni landscape with consistency and clarity.

Laddered strategies follow an approach where one invests in bonds spread out across different maturities. This helps to diversify potential interest rate risk by offering the opportunity to reset one’s portfolio as interest rates rise, while also retaining some exposure to lock in yield if rates fall. The end goal is to help provide consistency in interest rate exposure while offering a potentially more predictable experience for investors.

Laddering municipal bond portfolios has been a core investment approach used by advisors for many years, producing an effective solution to participate in the tax-advantaged fixed income space. WisdomTree has collaborated with Insight Investment Management to bring a laddered approach to the ETF industry. Our combined approach fuses the consistency offered by ladders with the active credit expertise of Insight, one of the longest serving municipal managers in the industry.

Combining an Active Approach with a Laddered Strategy in Munis

Municipal securities are debt issued by state and local government entities to fund general operations and finance revenue generating projects such as essential services (water/sewer), public utilities, roadways, transit/transportation and hospitals. As a result, the muni universe is made up of a large, wide variety of issuers which offer a diverse set of opportunities from a trading and credit perspective.

Diversifying exposure equally across maturity rungs through laddering, letting securities roll down the curve and reinvesting proceeds into higher maturities at the top of the ladder provides a consistent, all-weather approach to mitigating interest rate risk. The disciplined approach reduces the need to time interest rate changes while also providing flexibility to position for future opportunities to add value through security and sector selection.

The WisdomTree Core Laddered Municipal Fund (WTMU)

WTMU is designed to provide a laddered approach for exposure to investment-grade municipal bonds across the intermediate part of the municipal yield curve. It diversifies exposure across maturity rungs out to 15 years in securities that will mature or are likely to be called or tendered. As bonds begin to mature, get called or are sold off from shorter rungs, the proceeds are reinvested at the very top of the ladder in securities that are higher yielding, given the positive slope of the municipal yield curve.

“Laddered Approach”

The WisdomTree High Income Laddered Municipal Fund (WTMY)

WTMY combines a laddered approach across 15 years of the muni yield curve, providing exposure to lower-rated investment-grade securities with a targeted allocation to the high-yield sector. The Fund targets at least 80% in municipals rated A+ or below, or what we define as high-income municipals, and can invest up to 50% in securities deemed to be non-investment grade. This approach balances the pursuit of higher after-tax income with the desire for diversification, transparency and liquidity.

Both WTMU and WTMY's investment process provides a foundation for active credit selection. A disciplined risk management process is utilized to mitigate undue risk of the desired credit profile and seeks to isolate undervalued securities and sectors to exploit market inefficiencies through independent credit research and flexible opportunistic trading.

Munis: A Key Source of Value

For investors, municipals serve as a strong diversifier of assets, typically offer better credit quality than corporate bonds and can provide a more defensive behavior in rising rate environments, all while offering the opportunity for enhanced after-tax income.

Important Risks Related to this Article

There are risks associated with investing, including the possible loss of principal. Municipal securities carry various risks, including credit, interest rate, prepayment and valuation risks. Issuers may face financial difficulties that impact their ability to meet payment obligations. The value of these securities can fluctuate due to changes in revenue sources, local economic and political conditions, and industry-specific downturns (e.g., education, healthcare, transportation, utilities). Additionally, tax-exempt income from municipal securities could become taxable due to regulatory changes or issuer noncompliance, potentially reducing their value. Fixed income investments are subject to interest rate risk; their value will normally decline as interest rates rise. Higher-yield securities or “junk” bonds have lower credit ratings and involve a greater risk to principal. Fixed income investments are also subject to credit risk, the risk that the issuer of a bond will fail to pay interest and principal in a timely manner or that negative perceptions of the issuer’s ability to make such payments will cause the price of that bond to decline. While the Fund attempts to limit credit and counterparty exposure, the value of an investment in the Fund may change quickly and without warning in response to issuer or counterparty defaults and changes in the credit ratings of the Fund’s portfolio investments. Please read the Fund’s prospectus for specific details regarding the Fund’s risk profile.

Neither WisdomTree, Inc., nor its affiliates, nor Foreside Fund Services, LLC, nor its affiliates provide tax advice. All references to tax matters or information provided here are for illustrative purposes only and should not be considered tax advice and cannot be used for the purpose of avoiding tax penalties. Investors seeking tax advice should consult an independent tax advisor.

Kevin Flanagan, Head of Fixed Income Strategy

As part of WisdomTree's Investment Strategy group, Kevin serves as Head of Fixed Income Strategy. In this role, he contributes to the asset allocation team, writes fixed income-related content and travels with the sales team, conducting client-facing meetings and providing expertise on WisdomTree's existing and future bond ETFs. In addition, Kevin works closely with the fixed income team. Prior to joining WisdomTree, Kevin spent 30 years at Morgan Stanley, where he was Managing Director and Chief Fixed Income Strategist for Wealth Management. He was responsible for tactical and strategic recommendations and created asset allocation models for fixed income securities. He was a contributor to the Morgan Stanley Wealth Management Global Investment Committee, primary author of Morgan Stanley Wealth Management's monthly and weekly fixed income publications, and collaborated with the firm's Research and Consulting Group Divisions to build ETF and fund manager asset allocation models. Kevin has an MBA from Pace University's Lubin Graduate School of Business, and a B.S in Finance from Fairfield University.

Rick Harper serves as the Chief Investment Officer, Fixed Income and Model Portfolios at WisdomTree Asset Management, where he oversees the firm’s suite of fixed income and currency exchange-traded funds. He is also a voting member of the WisdomTree Model Portfolio Investment Committee and takes a leading role in the management and oversight of the fixed income model allocations. He plays an active role in risk management and oversight within the firm.

Rick has over 29 years investment experience in strategy and portfolio management positions at prominent investment firms. Prior to joining WisdomTree in 2007, Rick held senior level strategist roles with RBC Dain Rauscher, Bank One Capital Markets, ETF Advisors, and Nuveen Investments. At ETF Advisors, he was the portfolio manager and developer of some of the first fixed income exchange-traded funds. His research has been featured in leading periodicals including the Journal of Portfolio Management and the Journal of Indexes. He graduated from Emory University and earned his MBA at Indiana University.

Original Post

data.title: AGQ: Time To Punish Silver's Mr. Slammy
data.description:
With the backdrop of U.S. President Trump's "Liberation Day" tariffs announced on April 2nd, the global markets have turned to panic. At one point in early market trading, Crude Oil (CL1:COM) was down over 7%, the Nasdaq 100 (US100:IND) was down over 5%, and Gold (XAUUSD:CUR) was down over 3%. In the equity markets, the stocks that have been hit the hardest so far are those that have deeper connections to foreign supply chains. A notable example being Nike (NKE), which was down by as much as 13% shortly after the market opened on April 3rd.

Interestingly, the metal markets have been hit just as hard as the major indexes, with Silver (XAGUSD:CUR) down by as much as 6% before the equity markets opened on April 3rd. I think this is more indicative of an opportunistic slam of the metals than any justifiable selloff from tariff implications. And I'm expressing that belief by taking a leveraged Silver long through the ProShares Ultra Silver ETF (NYSEARCA:AGQ).

Tariff Impact On Silver?

First, let's look at the recently announced tariffs on bullion; actually, wait, they don't exist. Metals are exempt from the tariffs. Per Bloomberg:

The long list of exemptions to the cascade of duties captures all base metals, precious metals including gold, as well as niche materials. That means that tariffs on steel, aluminum and, eventually, copper won’t be stacked on top of the newly-announced country levies.

On one hand, we could attempt to argue that the recent rise we've seen in metals like Gold and Copper (HG1:COM) is due to market participants trying to secure resources ahead of tariffs. If true, that could certainly explain the weakness in those metals, since those resources aren't currently being included in the tariff plan.

Yet, even with Silver providing utility as an industrial metal, the price increase in Silver since Trump was elected in November hasn't been sustainably close to that of Gold or Copper. Given that, I don't think we can point to any hypothetical front-running of phantom tariffs on metal as the reason Silver has been pummeled on April 3rd. Rather, I think it's more indicative of an opportunistic slamming of Silver, given the diminishing bullion supply held at vaults like the LBMA.

Short Squeeze 2.0

I laid out my thesis about Silver Squeeze 2.0 in a prior article for Seeking Alpha that was focused on the Sprott Physical Silver Bullion Fund (PSLV). I encourage readers who haven't seen that article already to consider reading it. Here's the main takeaway:

Given the stress in the bullion market, my view is a large Silver short badly wants to keep PSLV trading at a discount to NAV. I suspect this short trade is going to fail miserably as forced covering pushes PSLV aggressively higher. I'm buying more.

That stress in the bullion market can be seen through the 4.5% month-over-month reduction in Silver vault holdings at the LBMA in February - a figure that now puts vault holdings down nearly 40% from highs in June 2021.

In the time since that article, not only have we seen the apparent shorting of PSLV continue, but it has seemingly intensified. FINRA Short Volume actually hit 30.3 million shares during the March 27th session, and the Short Volume Ratio hit 61.8 on April 2nd. Bears are trying very hard to keep a lid on this fund.

And again, this interest in PSLV is not happening in other Silver funds like the iShares Silver Trust (SLV) which is only doing about half the daily trade volume of PSLV even though that fund is nearly 3x the size of PSLV by AUM.

The ProShares Ultra Silver ETF

Pure and simple, I think these recent moves higher in the precious metals markets have been about far more than just repatriation from London. I'm very much of the view that Gold is moving primarily as a "risk-off," pristine collateral asset in a world with trillions of dollars in debt and derivatives. Silver has a role here as well. And while shares of AGQ are in no way a replacement for physical silver or funds like PSLV, I do think in a raw speculative sense, AGQ shares will perform quite well provided the price of Silver goes where I think it is going to go.

Fund Details AGQ ProShares Ultra Gold ETF (UGL)
Issuer ProShares ProShares
Inception 12/01/2008 12/01/2008
Expense Ratio 0.95% 0.95%
AUM $715.03M $472.61M

Source: Seeking Alpha

At 0.95%, the fund is more expensive than something like a Sprott Silver Bullion Trust or iShares Silver ETF, but it's in line with other leveraged products offered by ProShares. At a $715 million AUM, AGQ is actually a bit bigger than the sister fund for Gold. But the key here is remembering that AGQ is a leveraged product that aims to double the daily return of spot Silver prices. It is for tactical trading only and not for long-term investment purposes. Consider the 10-year performance of holding AGQ versus PSLV:

With PSLV shares nearly doubling in the last decade, the leveraged Silver product has only been able to manage an 8.5% total return. And that is with a strong rally in 2024. Prior to last year, the total return of AGQ was considerably negative. Thus, I have to stress again, something like AGQ should only be used for short-term trading. My personal view is we're going to see Silver quickly recover back to $34 per ounce within the next week or two. I suspect we could even see $40 silver before the end of June. I do plan to hold AGQ through April before reassessing my position.

Risks

Please review the risks of leveraged ETFs as explained in the linked SEC bulletin before placing any trades in this ETF. These risks include extreme volatility, time and volatility decay, and the real possibility of losing all of your investment. The normal risks can be exaggerated with single-commodity ETFs such as this. Only experienced traders who fully understand the risks should trade them. This article further explains the important risks of such ETFs with examples.

Closing Summary

Tariffs are rocking the global markets. The announcement from the White House is having an impact on equities, commodities, metals, and credit. The market is acting like Silver has been roaring in anticipation of future tariffs on the metal that do appear to be coming. Thus, Mr. Slammy is up to his old tricks with a massive pummeling of the metal before the market opened on April 3rd. Those of us who have been in this market for the last decade know how this is likely going to go. Opportunistic slams in Silver may be painful in the shorter term, but prices typically resolve higher shortly after. In a credit crunch, he who holds the paper does not make the rules. Nothing beats holding physical or even PSLV in a tax-advantaged account. But for some speculative swings, I expect AGQ will perform very well when the Silver bulls punish Mr. Slammy.

data.title: Trump's Reciprocal Tariffs: The Art Of Calculation
data.description:
Now we're officially entering the biggest trade war the world has known at least in a century. Investors should expect an avalanche of emotional commentaries from all sides, but emotions rarely (if ever) help in investing. Thus, I keep my primary goal simple: examining the potential consequences of the new tariffs and adjusting an investment strategy.

What We Know For Now

1. Some key exporters are about to be hit with extremely high tariffs: Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent confirmed that China will get a 54% cumulative tariff rate (the 20% tariff rate that was introduced earlier will be combined with 34% reciprocal tariffs). The European Union will face a 20% rate, and Vietnam and Taiwan will get 46% and 32% respectively.

It's quite logical that the US wants to decouple from China amid a growing geopolitical confrontation. However, slapping more tariffs on Vietnam, India, and other key partners who help the US in this decoupling process means that US companies who moved their manufacturing capacities from China to the aforementioned, less geopolitically exposed countries, are now in just as bad position as if they simply stayed in China. That doesn't look like a prudent economic incentive, in my view.

2. Markets around the world are sharply down: S&P 500 futures are down by more than 3% as of the time of writing, sharply dipping below 5500. As I've noted in my previous article on tariffs, the level of 5500 is important from a technical perspective.

If we don't see any positive news in the next few days, a further decline in the US market looks probable.

3. Tariffs seem to be calculated in a weird way: As some folks have already investigated, reciprocal tariffs were calculated by simply dividing a country’s US trade surplus by their US exports.

To get an idea of how mathematically inconsistent this approach is: the US has a noticeable trade surplus, not a trade deficit with Australia. Even still, Australia was slapped with a "basic" 10% tariff, and we're only about to know what the Trump administration going to do about this.

A bit later after the tariffs announcement, the official methodology of tariff calculation was published by the Office of the US Trade Representative (USTR).

According to the USTR:

Consider an environment in which the U.S. levies a tariff of rate τ_i on country i and ∆τ_i reflects the change in the tariff rate. Let ε<0 represent the elasticity of imports with respect to import prices, let φ>0 represent the passthrough from tariffs to import prices, let m_i>0 represent total imports from country i, and let x_i>0 represent total exports. Then the decrease in imports due to a change in tariffs equals ∆τ_i*ε*φ*m_i<0.

Here's what each part means:

  • Δτ_i is the change in tariff rate that the US imposes on a country i.
  • x_i represents the total exports from the US to a country i.
  • m_i represents the total imports to the US from a country i.
  • ε (epsilon) is a negative number that tells us how imports respond to price changes (elasticity).
  • φ (phi) is how much tariffs affect import prices (passthrough).

In simple terms, this formula calculates how much the US should adjust its tariff rate on a country based on whether the US has a trade deficit or surplus with a particular country.

However, a "reverse engineering" of the formula shows a 100% linear chart, which results from simply dividing a country’s US trade surplus by their US exports, an approach I've mentioned earlier above.

Given that the official formula has five variables, including elasticity, it's extremely unlikely that we could get the same perfectly linear chart by using it. Thus, for me, it looks like folks who were responsible for calculating the tariffs took a rather simplistic approach but pretended to use a more sophisticated way of calculation.

4. The chances of disputing Trump's decision on tariffs look very slim: Currently, the US Senate voted to undo the 25 percent tariffs on Canadian goods, which looks like a mostly symbolic move as the resolution is unlikely to be approved in the House of Representatives. The initial legislation to lift tariffs against Canada was initially proposed in mid-March, and regardless of the outcome, lifting these tariffs will be a drop in the ocean compared to the reciprocal tariffs.

To repeal the new tariffs, we would need to see extraordinary unanimity on this issue from both Republicans and Democrats in both houses of Congress, but I seriously doubt that is something we can expect in the foreseeable future.

5. It's unknown how harsh the response from China and other countries will be: In an interview with Fox News, Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent warned from retaliating:

"My advice to every country right now is do not retaliate. Sit back, take it in, let's see how it goes. Because if you retaliate, there will be escalation...If you don't retaliate, this is the high-water mark."

In the meantime, China urges to cancel the new tariffs and prepares for retaliation, though it's unclear what the exact retaliatory measures will be. The EU is also preparing countermeasures, but the suspension of the EU's retaliatory tariffs stays active until April 14, so it doesn't seem that we'll see an immediate retaliation from the EU.

What To Do Now?

If putting the world on the verge of a global trade war is just an attempt to re-negotiate trade on better terms, that's a recipe for a potential stagflationary recession or a potential win for Trump. In case of a swift reversal of tariffs (let's say, until the end of April), there will be a solid chance of market recovery later in May.

However, if all this is being done with the real intent of replacing taxes with tariffs assuming that tariffs can be a viable alternative to taxes (and tariffs, in fact, are a tax on the consumption of imported goods), then that's a recipe for an economic agony for years to come, without any chance of "winning". According to new estimates from the Yale Budget Lab, the tariffs would result in a 2.3 percentage point rise in overall inflation this year, costing the average household approximately $3,800. I strongly recommend reading the whole piece as there are plenty of interesting numbers and charts.

I'm not a fan of catching falling knives in such a market, especially given that we haven't seen anything like this in a very long time. Gold ($GLD and $GDX) and short-term bonds ($SGOV) remain more relevant than ever. I've already discussed hedging against tariffs in this article, so feel free to take a look.

Braver folks may try to start buying the dip in stocks now if you believe in a swift resolution of the trade war, and more risk-averse investors may wait until April 9, when there will be more clarity on potential retaliation and trade talks to reduce the tariffs. The optimal strategy, in my opinion, is to gradually increase hedges in a portfolio the longer the tariffs stay active and the recession risk rises.

I'll keep publishing updates on the matter, so stay tuned.
